meta.title: Iran: Regime Continuity, or Slide Toward Collapse?
meta.subtitle: Seven-variable status board · Three time anchors · Evolution paths

source_report.title: Four Trajectories for the Iranian Regime and the Second-Order Geopolitical Transmission Across the Middle East

meta.stance_summary:
**Procedurally the succession is done; in practice the regime is incapacitated.** After the joint US–Israeli decapitation strike on February 28, 2026 killed Khamenei, his second son Mojtaba Khamenei (56) was selected on March 8 by the Assembly of Experts (under heavy IRGC pressure) as the new Supreme Leader. On March 11, Grand Ayatollah Sistani — the most-followed marja in the Shia world — issued a carefully worded congratulation (well-wishing, not endorsement). **Mojtaba has not appeared in public since February 28**: all statements are read by state television, his injuries remain unverified, and he transmits written orders via couriers.

In effect the present state is **Mojtaba in name + IRGC in fact** — visible in Iran's maximalist negotiating posture toward the US: recognition of Iranian sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, full sanctions lifting, war reparations, and unfreezing of all overseas assets.

**Iran's regime trajectory does not depend on internal dynamics alone but on the tension among seven mutually constraining variables**: Israeli strategic goals, China–Russia strategic patience, Iran's internal power structure, US Navy actual capacity at Hormuz, US domestic politics, Israeli unilateral escalation potential, and Iran's red lines.

**Three time anchors set the rhythm of evolution**: the Israeli Knesset election by 2026-10-27, the US midterm elections on 2026-11-03, and the end of Trump's term on 2028-01-20.

glossary.note: Iran's core armed force, commanded by Hossein Salami. Comprises five service branches (ground forces, navy, aerospace force, Quds Force for overseas special operations, and the Basij militia) plus 17 provincial corps. Through its commercial empire it controls roughly 30-40% of Iranian GDP.

The highest rank of Shia clerical authority, meaning 'source of emulation.' Under the Shia constitution, the Supreme Leader must hold this rank. There are roughly 8 active top-tier marjas today; Sistani is the de facto highest global authority.
Khamenei's eldest son. Declared successor in March 2026, but lacks marja qualification, leaving a constitutional grey zone over his legitimacy.
Global Shia Islam's de facto highest marja. Age 96, resident in Najaf, Iraq. Final arbiter of Mojtaba's legitimacy.
Codename for the February 2026 Israeli military operation against Iran. The decapitation strike killed Khamenei.
Codename for the June 2025 Israeli military operation against Iran, also known as the '12-Day War.' Destroyed 70% of Iranian ballistic missile launchers and 92% of its navy.
Iran's official currency. Pre-war rate was 1 USD = 42,000 rials; by May 2026 it had fallen past 1:90,000.
Iran's current president, elected July 2024 as a reformist. Does not come from the IRGC and commands no military force.
Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia and the kingdom's de facto chief decision-maker.
Southwestern Iranian province with a large Arab population. Contains roughly 80% of Iran's oil reserves.

history_compare.title: 1991 Soviet Collapse Comparison
history_compare.intro: The 1991 Soviet collapse was not a sudden event — it was the accumulation of specific nodes across six years from 1985 to 1991. Iran's disintegration today, if it occurs, will also not be a single event but the accumulation of nodes. From Chernobyl to the Soviet collapse took five and a half years; from Operation Rising Lion to a possible Iranian regime collapse, the window could be 4-6 years.

mapping.soviet: 1985-03 Gorbachev takes power, introduces Glasnost and Perestroika

mapping.iran: Operation Rising Lion / Operation Epic Fury (nuclear facilities struck plus Khamenei's death)

mapping.soviet: 1989-11 Fall of the Berlin Wall (external support network collapses)
mapping.iran: Russia does not protect Iran (Hanna Notte 2025-06)
Kurds (PJAK, the most organized) likely to be first to formally secede
Khuzestan or Sistan-Baluchistan provincial IRGC corps publicly dissents

mapping.soviet: 1991-12 Formal dissolution of the Soviet Union
mapping.iran: Disintegration (if it occurs) — expected 4-6 year window

history_compare.key_difference: The Soviet slow-collapse path worked fundamentally because Gorbachev voluntarily let go — during the 1989 East European revolutions he explicitly ordered Soviet forces not to intervene, abandoning the traditional Soviet option of repression. Iran today has no Gorbachev-equivalent figure: Pezeshkian is a reformist, but he is not IRGC-born and commands no force. With no one inside the IRGC willing to let go, Iran's slow-collapse process is likely to skip directly to disintegration. This is the fundamental reason slow collapse is given only 12-15%.

time_anchors.label: Israeli Knesset Election
time_anchors.short_desc: Survival pressure on the Netanyahu coalition
time_anchors.description: Israel's Knesset must hold its election by October 27, 2026. Current polling has Netanyahu's coalition at 52 seats (61 needed for a majority); Finance Minister Smotrich's Religious Zionism party may fall below the electoral threshold. Netanyahu's personal 2027 criminal trial approaches, requiring a 'wartime leader' narrative. This creates strong incentive to take a visible 'decisive' Iran action before and around the election.

time_anchors.label: US Midterm Elections
time_anchors.description: US midterms are November 3, 2026. The forecasting model projects Republicans losing about 28 House seats (Democrats need only +3 net to flip). The probability of losing the Senate is also non-trivial (even-money). Trump's Iran war approval has fallen from 78% (June 2025 at war's outbreak) to 41% (April 2026), with 63% of voters calling the war wrong. A midterm defeat would put Trump in effective lame-duck status for the final 14 months of his term.

time_anchors.label: End of Trump's Term
time_anchors.short_desc: Window of US full backing for Israel closes
time_anchors.description: Trump's second term ends January 20, 2028. Israel sees this as the 'last reliably aligned' US presidency — no successor (Vance / Rubio / Don Jr / a Democrat) is likely to back Israel as Trump has on Iran. Probability of Israeli unilateral escalation of some form during the November 2026 – January 2028 window: roughly 50–65%.

variable_groups.label: Structural Background
variable_groups.intro: These are long-term pressures that don't change much over a few years.

variables.label: Israeli Strategic Goals
variables.short_desc: Israel wants to eliminate Iran's nuclear program, ballistic missiles, and proxy network
variables.current_state: Standing long-term pressure; does not change with administration shifts

preconditions.label: Iran's 1979 revolution embedded 'destroy Israel' in regime ideology
preconditions.analysis: The Iranian Islamic Republic built 'opposition to Israel' into a core legitimacy pillar after 1979. This is not rhetoric — Iran's Supreme Leader, IRGC, and clerical system have reiterated it across 1979–2024. The ideological commitment frames Israel's response: 'de-capability of Iran' is treated as an existential matter.

preconditions.label: Iran's proxy network as a persistent threat to Israel
preconditions.analysis: Hezbollah (created with IRGC help in 1982), Hamas (from 2007 in Gaza), the Iraqi Shia militias (rose after 2003), and Yemen's Houthis (from 2014) form four fronts. After the October 7, 2023 attacks the four fronts activated in concert; the 2024 strikes — Nasrallah killed, Assad regime fallen, pager attacks — degraded the network but did not eliminate it.

preconditions.label: Iran's two direct strikes on Israel in 2024
preconditions.analysis: 'True Promise I' in April 2024 (~300 missiles + drones) and 'True Promise II' in October 2024 (~180–200 ballistic missiles) made Israel recognize that even with proxies weakened, Iran's own missile capability is now a direct existential threat. This fundamentally changed the Iran–Israel contest.

preconditions.label: IAEA verification framework broken + enriched uranium missing
preconditions.analysis: On October 18, 2025, the IAEA reported it has 'no information' on Iran's enriched uranium stockpile — meaning Iran moved the material during the 12-Day War. With the IAEA unable to verify, Israel sees no mechanism for 'verified de-nuclearization,' forcing reliance on direct military means.

preconditions.label: Netanyahu's roughly 50% probability of prosecution in 2027
preconditions.analysis: Netanyahu's corruption trial is expected to deliver verdicts in 2027. His political survival depends on the 'wartime leader' narrative — as long as Iran remains an existential threat he can hold the coalition together. This adds a domestic 'stall + escalate' incentive.

mechanism.label: Combination of military + assassination + diplomatic pressure
mechanism.analysis: Israel's toolkit: (1) direct military strikes on nuclear and missile facilities (Operation Rising Lion in June 2025 + Operation Epic Fury in February 2026); (2) Mossad assassinations (nuclear scientists + senior IRGC + Haniyeh in Tehran + Nasrallah in Beirut); (3) cyber attacks + Mossad infiltration; (4) pressure on Iran through US (snapback, joint operations); (5) dismantling of the proxy network (Hezbollah in September 2024 / Assad regime falls December 2024). Israel's 'kill any successor' threat is a leverage tool, not the strategic goal itself — if any Iranian regime accepts de-nuclearization, de-missilization, and proxy cutoff, Israel will let Mojtaba live.

consequences.label: Full de-capability + Iran accepts (ideal; 10–15%)
consequences.analysis: Mojtaba or a successor accepts permanent de-nuclearization + missile limits + proxy cutoff. Very difficult — this equates to regime legitimacy suicide. Requires extreme pressure (economic collapse + pragmatic faction rises) to achieve.

consequences.label: Partial de-capability + ongoing friction (realistic path; 40–50%)
consequences.analysis: Iran accepts partial inspections + halts high-level enrichment + ambiguous commitments on proxies, in exchange for partial sanctions relief. Israel accepts but reserves the 'we act alone if you breach' posture. This is the most likely Phase 1–2 outcome.

consequences.label: Israeli unilateral escalation + incomplete de-nuclearization (30–40%)
consequences.analysis: Within the lame-duck window Israel judges 'we must act while Trump is still in office' and conducts large-scale unilateral actions — assassinations, missile-site strikes, cyber attacks. But capability constraints mean Fordow cannot be reached. Result: Iranian nuclear residue + ongoing friction.

consequences.label: Extreme action + Smotrich pushed (3–5%)
consequences.analysis: Smotrich/Ben-Gvir extremists push Israel toward actions beyond convention — large-scale ground action, or use of weapons of mass destruction (Wilkerson's extreme hypothesis). The international reaction would be uncontrollable, including Gulf states publicly turning.

observations.label: Smotrich / Ben-Gvir publicly propose extreme options in the cabinet
observations.threshold: Open proposal of WMD use against Iran → severe US-Israel alliance strain
observations.current_state: Not publicly proposed (privately pushed)

observations.label: IAEA regains full verification of enriched uranium
observations.threshold: IAEA confirms full inspection access → negotiation credibility increases
observations.current_state: Has not happened (IAEA still in the dark since 2025-10-18)

observations.label: Israel unilaterally strikes Iranian nuclear facilities again
observations.threshold: Any unilateral strike → enters escalation phase
observations.current_state: Has not happened during ceasefire

observations.label: Israel–Saudi formal normalization resumes
observations.threshold: MBS publicly meets Netanyahu → regional realignment signal
observations.current_state: Frozen (paused after October 7, 2023; not resumed)

variables.label: China–Russia Strategic Patience + Latent Escalation
variables.short_desc: Both gain from the US being bogged down in the Middle East
variables.current_state: Strategic waiting + gradual escalation of arms and intelligence

preconditions.label: China's 25-year, $400B Iran deal signed in 2021
preconditions.analysis: The 25-year comprehensive cooperation agreement signed in 2021 commits China to ~$400B in investment in return for below-market Iranian oil. The agreement binds China's economic interests to Iran's. China receives ~13% of its oil imports from Iran.

preconditions.label: Russia needs Iran as an anti-US strategic anchor
preconditions.analysis: Russia has deepened its reliance on Iran as an anti-US bloc partner since 2014 Crimea, and even more so after 2022 Ukraine — Iran supplies Shahed drones to Russia, Russia supplies technology to Iran.

preconditions.label: mBridge + CIPS financial pipes are operational
preconditions.analysis: mBridge (multilateral CBDC, China + Hong Kong + Thailand + UAE + Saudi Arabia) routes 95% of Iran–China settlement in RMB. CIPS provides the clearing backend. This gives Iran a financial survival path once cut off from SWIFT.

preconditions.label: Ukraine war limits Russia's ability to engage on Iran
preconditions.analysis: Russia's military bandwidth is consumed by Ukraine; it cannot directly intervene in Iran. So it must support indirectly — arms supply + intelligence sharing + UN Security Council veto.

mechanism.label: Strategic patience + latent escalation, no direct military engagement
mechanism.analysis: Both Beijing and Moscow practice 'strategic patience': let the US bog down in the Middle East while keeping Iran afloat with limited aid. Concretely: China delivered HQ-9B SAM batteries to Iran in July 2025; Russia agreed to supply Verba MANPADS in December 2025; both share intelligence helping Iran target US bases; mBridge keeps Iran's economy minimally operational; and both veto any escalating UNSC sanctions. Neither will intervene militarily — both fear direct US confrontation and don't want to lose oil price benefits.

consequences.label: Patience continues; Iran limps along (50–60%)
consequences.analysis: Base case: China and Russia maintain current tempo — intel + limited arms + financial pipes. Iran's economy is heavily stressed but does not collapse. Both gain from sustained oil prices + ongoing US resource drain.

consequences.label: China-Russia upgrade to substantive support, Iran rebounds (15–20%)
consequences.analysis: If Iran nears collapse, China may launch a large-scale arms shipment (CNN April reporting: 'preparing'). Russia may send military advisors. This would shift the Israel-Iran balance and force the US-Israel side to reconsider strategy.

consequences.label: China-Russia split appears; Iran loses support (5–10%)
consequences.analysis: China and Russia's interests in Iran are not identical — China values oil + long-term strategy more; Russia needs the anti-US bloc intact. If an event splits them publicly (e.g., Ukraine ceasefire + Russian pivot), Iran loses key backing.

consequences.label: Large-scale Chinese arms shipment to Iran (15–20%)
consequences.analysis: CNN's April 2026 reporting on US intel: China is 'preparing further arms shipments.' If realized — J-10C fighters, HQ-22 SAMs, ballistic-missile components — Iran's confidence rises and negotiations become harder.

observations.label: Large-scale Chinese arms shipment to Iran
observations.threshold: Shipments of J-10C / HQ-22 / ballistic-missile components → global energy market shock
observations.current_state: US intel reports 'preparing' but not yet delivered

observations.label: Russia sends military advisors or public technical support
observations.threshold: Public announcement → direct US-Russia confrontation risk rises
observations.current_state: Intelligence sharing has occurred; military advisors not announced

observations.label: mBridge actual cross-border settlement volume disclosed
observations.threshold: Public data showing Iran-China settlement >$100B annualized → de-dollarization narrative strengthens
observations.current_state: No specific figures disclosed

variable_groups.label: Current State
variable_groups.intro: These are 'hard constraints' that have already settled and won't change in the short term.

variables.label: Iran's Internal Power Structure
variables.short_desc: Mojtaba in name vs IRGC in fact

preconditions.label: Mojtaba's succession process completed (2026-03-08)
preconditions.analysis: The Assembly of Experts voted (under heavy IRGC pressure) to confirm Mojtaba as Supreme Leader. This satisfies the constitutional procedure. But the IRGC's pressure on Assembly members deprives the succession of the 'IRGC + clerical + political' three-way consensus that legitimized Khamenei's 1989 ascent.

preconditions.label: Sistani's well-wishing message (2026-03-11)
preconditions.analysis: The world's highest-following Shia marja Sistani sent a carefully measured congratulation — but **not an endorsement**. He did not confirm Mojtaba's standing as a qualified jurist. Sistani did not address Mojtaba's religious rank, only praying he would lead Iran 'against ill-wishers.' This is qualified support, not full religious legitimacy.

preconditions.label: Mojtaba has not been seen in public since 2-28
preconditions.analysis: After being wounded in the joint US-Israeli strike of February 28, Mojtaba has not appeared publicly — no video, no recent photographs. Two statements have been read by state television. Reports on his injuries range from 'minor fractures' to unofficial accounts of 'prosthetic leg, multiple surgeries, period of unconsciousness' — none verifiable.

preconditions.label: IRGC commander Hossein Salami leads in practice
preconditions.analysis: Salami has been IRGC commander since 2019. During Mojtaba's incapacitation he is effectively the senior decision-maker. The relationships among Salami, Quds Force commander Esmail Qaani, and Mojtaba will shape IRGC's internal politics.

mechanism.label: Mojtaba in name + IRGC in fact + courier communications
mechanism.analysis: Current operation: Mojtaba is Supreme Leader in name, IRGC drives decisions. All statements come via state TV reading; Mojtaba transmits written orders to negotiators through couriers. This 'paralyzed nominal leader' state has no Iranian historical precedent — 1989's succession had IRGC + clerical + political consensus; 1979 was direct revolutionary installation. The current arrangement is unstable because Mojtaba's health could deteriorate, and IRGC sub-factions (Salami / Qaani / Basij / Navy) may compete for control during his prolonged absence. The maximalist negotiating posture (Hormuz sovereignty + full sanctions lifting + reparations) reflects IRGC hardliners' grip on the talks.

consequences.label: Mojtaba recovers and rules substantively (15–25%)
consequences.analysis: If Mojtaba's injuries heal enough for public appearances, meetings, and audiences with foreign leaders, he can gradually reclaim decision authority from IRGC. But that requires IRGC to cede ground — Salami and others won't yield easily.

consequences.label: Mojtaba remains incapacitated; IRGC takes over substantively (40–50%)
consequences.analysis: Base case: Mojtaba never returns to full function; IRGC operates everything in his name. This 'militarization with a clerical façade' structure can persist 1–3 years.

consequences.label: Mojtaba dies → second succession crisis (15–20%)
consequences.analysis: Mojtaba dies of injuries or in a second assassination attempt. Iran restarts the succession process — without the March 2026 'emergency consensus' window. Candidates: Khamenei's elder son (not politically active), IRGC-picked non-family successor, revolutionary marja. Each option has legitimacy problems.

consequences.label: IRGC internal split breaks into the open (10–15%)
consequences.analysis: IRGC's five branches (Army / Air Force / Navy / Strategic Missile Forces / Basij) may openly clash during Mojtaba's prolonged absence. Quds Force wants to keep supporting proxies / Navy wants to keep the Hormuz lever / Basij wants concentrated internal control. Any public split dramatically lowers regime stability.

observations.label: Mojtaba appears on public video
observations.threshold: Public appearance / video / meeting a foreign leader → substantive governance capacity restored
observations.current_state: Has not occurred (over two months silent)

observations.label: IRGC branches publicly diverge
observations.threshold: Sepah News reports contradictory positions across branches → factionalism surfaces
observations.current_state: No public divergence observed

observations.label: Mojtaba dies suddenly (health or assassination)
observations.threshold: Any form of death → immediate entry into second succession crisis
observations.current_state: Has not occurred (but injury rumors persist)

variables.label: US Navy Actual Hormuz Capacity
variables.short_desc: 5th Fleet can restore only ~10% of pre-war Strait flow
variables.current_state: Escort capacity severely limited; 68 tankers stranded

cross_page_link.label: → Jump to supply-chain US Navy hub

preconditions.label: 5th Fleet headquartered in Bahrain
preconditions.analysis: The US Fifth Fleet is headquartered at Naval Support Activity Bahrain in Manama. It is responsible for US naval operations in the Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Red Sea, and western Indian Ocean — the only US theater fleet dedicated to the Middle East.

preconditions.label: Insufficient destroyers in current deployment
preconditions.analysis: Escort convoys of 5–10 ships require 8–10 destroyers cycling continuously. The 5th Fleet's current deployment lacks that scale — most destroyers are rotating, in maintenance, or assigned elsewhere (including Red Sea Houthi defense).

preconditions.label: 68 fully loaded tankers stranded in the Persian Gulf
preconditions.analysis: CNN (May 2026): 68 fully loaded tankers, holding roughly 16 billion liters of crude, are awaiting either US Navy-confirmed safe escort or Iran's terms of passage.

preconditions.label: Interceptor stockpiles depleted by 45–50%
preconditions.analysis: CSIS Cancian 'Last Rounds' report: US support to Israel has consumed roughly 45–50% of pre-war stockpiles of PrSM / THAAD / Patriot. Any naval escalation (e.g., another carrier group) further depletes resources originally allocated to Indo-Pacific deterrence.

mechanism.label: Selective escort + extended deterrence
mechanism.analysis: 5th Fleet's actual operations: (1) selective escort of a few high-value tankers (2 tankers + 1 destroyer per transit with satellite + air coverage); (2) Operation Epic Escort under evaluation (Pentagon began studying full escort options in March 2026); (3) priority coordination with Gulf navies (US + Saudi + UAE navies possibly cooperating); (4) extended deterrence (Trump publicly said 'the US will act if Qatar facilities are hit again'). Hegseth publicly called the escort 'a gift to the world,' but CNN reported directly in May that 'the US Navy isn't escorting commercial ships' — showing the gap between political messaging and operational reality. The 45–50% ammo depletion makes any naval escalation costly.

consequences.label: Maintain ~10% throughput (base case; 50–60%)
consequences.analysis: Current state persists — selective escort keeps minimum commercial traffic flowing. The 68 stranded tankers gradually release in batches. Brent in the $80–100 range. Supply chains stressed but absorbable.

consequences.label: Add carrier group → 20–30% throughput (20–30%)
consequences.analysis: US redeploys 1–2 carrier strike groups from Indo-Pacific or Mediterranean to the 5th Fleet AOR, lifting Hormuz escort capacity to 20–30% of pre-war flow. This publicizes the 'Indo-Pacific vs Middle East' resource trade and substantively weakens Taiwan deterrence.

consequences.label: Further ammo depletion → drawdown (10–15%)
consequences.analysis: If Iran escalates and ammo consumption continues, DoD internal pressure may push 5th Fleet toward self-defense only, leaving Gulf commercial shipping dependent on Gulf states' own capacity — which they lack.

consequences.label: Full strikes restart → ports targeted (5–10%)
consequences.analysis: Trump enforces the 'US will act' threat; 5th Fleet escalates to combat operations. Bahrain base, UAE's Fujairah, Saudi facilities become Iranian targets. In this path Gulf commercial shipping fully collapses.

observations.label: 5th Fleet receives an additional carrier strike group
observations.threshold: Redeploying 1+ carrier group → escalation commitment signal
observations.current_state: Not yet occurred (under discussion)

observations.label: Recovery of commercial tanker flow through Hormuz
observations.threshold: Weekly transits from <20 ships up to >50 → confidence recovering
observations.current_state: Currently <20/day (under 11% of pre-war 178)

observations.label: US Navy's public statement on escort capacity
observations.threshold: Formal escort plan vs 'no available resources' statement
observations.current_state: Currently 'no available resources for full escort'

variable_groups.label: Short-Term Action Variables
variable_groups.intro: These are variables that will trigger or shift within a few months.

variables.label: US Domestic Politics
variables.short_desc: Midterms + Vance/Rubio for 2028 + Hegseth as fall guy
variables.current_state: Trump approval at 37% (second-term low) + pushing face-saving deal

preconditions.label: Trump approval at historic lows (37% overall / 41% Iran war / 30% inflation)
preconditions.analysis: Trump's overall approval has hit a second-term low of 37% (Foreign Policy Journal 5/19). Iran war approval 41%. Inflation approval 30%. 63% of voters say the war was wrong (73% of independents). This is voter sentiment 5.5 months out from the midterms.

preconditions.label: GOP projected to lose ~28 House seats
preconditions.analysis: Forecasting models project Republicans losing about 28 House seats (Democrats need only +3 net to flip). 33 GOP retirees vs. 21 Democratic retirees. Bannon's internal call: 'hold the House, lose the Senate.' Independent forecasters (Brookings et al.): 'lose the House likely; Senate even-money.'

preconditions.label: Vance leads Iran negotiations + bears the 'soft' label
preconditions.analysis: Trump assigned Iran negotiation to Vance — the same man who in 2024 repeatedly said he 'opposed new wars.' Vance now publicly backs the war while privately doubting it (Slate). His 2028 frontrunner standing is eroding.

preconditions.label: Hegseth set as the fall guy
preconditions.analysis: Hegseth's lie about a dead US soldier's father became public. Media (Slate, April) cast him as 'the fall guy if the war ends badly.' This preserves Trump's option to 'replace Hegseth and walk away clean.'

preconditions.label: Rubio repositions as 'mature realist'
preconditions.analysis: Rubio's May remark to reporters — 'Trump attacked Iran because of Israel' — pushes responsibility onto the Netanyahu coalition. He is creating space to reposition as a 'mature foreign policy realist.' Slate reports Rubio's 2028 odds rising.

preconditions.label: Carlson's NYT interview publicly defending Vance
preconditions.analysis: In May 2026 Carlson openly defended Vance ('I couldn't be a bigger fan of him as a man') while slamming Trump and Rubio. This builds the 'paleo + post-liberal vs. hawks' new political structure.

mechanism.label: Trump pushes a face-saving deal to relieve midterm pressure
mechanism.analysis: Trump's current play: push a face-saving deal so that before the midterms he can declare 'we ended the Iran nuclear threat.' (1) Vance leads the talks, absorbing the 'soft' label and giving Trump a fall guy. (2) Hegseth stays in place as a fall guy. (3) Rubio repositions as 'mature realist,' future-arming a 2028 primary attack on Vance from the moderate side. Hawks (Hegseth / Rubio / Miller) vs. paleos (Bannon / Carlson / Massie) vs. post-liberals (Vance) form a three-way tension. This is Trump's classic 'let the heirs burn each other out on hot assignments' move — no successor can consolidate easily.

consequences.label: Face-saving deal closes + Trump declares victory (35–45%)
consequences.analysis: Trump pushes Mojtaba (pre-midterms) into partial IAEA inspection + suspended high-level enrichment + ambiguous proxy concessions. Trump declares 'we ended the Iran nuclear threat.' Brent 80–100. Limited positive midterm effect.

consequences.label: Negotiations collapse + war restart (15–25%)
consequences.analysis: Iran's maximalist stance holds, or Israeli unilateral escalation kills the deal. Trump enforces 'US will act' threat, restarts large-scale operations. Brent spikes to 130–150 short-term. GOP midterm prospects further hurt.

consequences.label: Midterm defeat + Trump lame duck (50–60%)
consequences.analysis: GOP loses House (baseline 60–70% probability) → Trump paralyzed for 14 months. Iran policy challenged by Congress but pushed through by executive. Democratic House launches investigation of Hegseth, Project 2025 NEA purge.

consequences.label: Hegseth fired + cabinet reshuffle (15–25%)
consequences.analysis: Hegseth ousted as fall guy. Potential successors: Steve Feinberg (current deputy under-secretary, PE background), or a J6 committee member. The reshuffle deepens hawk faction's internal divisions.

observations.label: Midterm election results (2026-11-03)
observations.threshold: GOP loses House → Trump lame duck
observations.current_state: 5.5 months out; 60% probability of losing the House

observations.label: Vance makes a specific public statement on Iran policy
observations.threshold: Public opposition to current line → MAGA internal split becomes public
observations.current_state: Publicly supports + privately doubts

observations.label: Hegseth is fired or reassigned
observations.threshold: Reassignment → hawk faction's internal crack surfaces
observations.current_state: Fall guy setup in place but not executed

variables.label: Israeli Unilateral Escalation
variables.current_state: Pre-election pressure + lame-duck window opens time

preconditions.label: Netanyahu coalition only at 52 seats (61 needed)
preconditions.analysis: Current Knesset polling: Netanyahu's coalition projected at only 52 seats (61 needed for a majority). The coalition must hold elections by 2026-10-27. Finance Minister Smotrich's Religious Zionism party may fall below the threshold — the coalition is reportedly considering lowering the threshold to save Smotrich.

preconditions.label: 61% of Israelis oppose the ceasefire
preconditions.analysis: INSS Tel Aviv University polling: 61% of Israelis oppose the current Iran ceasefire. 51–56% opposed the initial two-week ceasefire. This is a domestic pressure the Netanyahu coalition cannot ignore — voters demand 'decisive ending.'

preconditions.label: US lame-duck window opens time
preconditions.analysis: Trump likely enters effective lame-duck status after the 2026-11-03 midterms (baseline 60% probability of losing the House). Israel knows this is the 'last reliably aligned' US presidency — no 2028 successor (Vance / Rubio / a Democrat) will support Israel's full Iran action the way Trump does.

preconditions.label: Israel lacks B-2 + GBU-57 (cannot reach Fordow)
preconditions.analysis: Israel unilaterally can: assassinate (Mossad + IAF), strike missile production sites (ground, low-altitude), conduct large-scale cyber attacks, hit remaining proxies. **Cannot**: destroy Fordow's deep enrichment site (only US B-2 + GBU-57 reach it), sustain prolonged air campaign (interceptor stockpiles depleted by war), block Hormuz (requires US Navy).

preconditions.label: Netanyahu's 2027 prosecution risk
preconditions.analysis: Netanyahu's corruption case is expected to deliver verdicts in 2027. His political survival depends on the 'wartime leader' narrative — must keep Iran perceived as a threat or the coalition loses its raison d'être.

mechanism.label: Target-class-limited escalation
mechanism.analysis: Israeli unilateral escalation is **target-class-limited** — selective against personnel, ground facilities, proxies, but cannot reach the actual core of Iran's nuclear program (Fordow). Type matrix: (1) Assassinations of IRGC seniors + nuclear scientists (proven, can continue); (2) Strikes on Iran missile production sites (ground, low-altitude, proven); (3) Large-scale cyber attacks (ongoing); (4) Strikes on remaining proxies (rebuilding Hezbollah, Iraqi militias); (5) Limited ground action at borders (Lebanon south, Golan). **Cannot do**: destroy Fordow, block Hormuz, sustain large-scale campaign. Timing logic: before the Knesset election (2026-08 to 10-27) needs visible 'decisive' action to push the coalition over the threshold; in the lame-duck window (2026-11 to 2028-01) judgment shifts to 'must finish de-capability while Trump is still here,' amplifying unilateral action.

consequences.label: Limited unilateral action before the Knesset election (25–35%)
consequences.analysis: Israel acts between 2026-08 and 2026-10 — assassinations + missile-site strikes. Aim: visible decisiveness ahead of the vote so the coalition crosses the Knesset threshold. Limited scale, does not break the deal framework. Possible forms: assassinations of senior IRGC (Salami, Qaani), strikes on Iranian missile lines, cyber attacks on nuclear-linked infrastructure.

consequences.label: Major lame-duck-window escalation (25–35%)
consequences.analysis: After Trump loses the House (2026-11+), Israel judges 'must act while a reliable US president is still in office,' driving larger unilateral action. Possible forms: repeated large-scale strikes + simultaneous diplomatic pressure on Iran + arming Iranian Kurds (which would push Turkey into crisis).

consequences.label: 2027 major trigger → seek renewed US involvement (15–25%)
consequences.analysis: Triggers like IAEA confirming Iran has restarted enrichment + Mojtaba openly violating the deal + Iran testing a device push Israel hard for renewed US joint action. Trump's lame-duck decision dynamics make US following Israel notably more likely.

consequences.label: Extreme action + Smotrich pushed (3–5%)
consequences.analysis: Smotrich/Ben-Gvir extremists push Israel to actions beyond convention — WMD use against Iran (Wilkerson hypothesis), or large-scale ground action in proxy regions. International reaction uncontrollable, including Gulf states publicly turning.

observations.label: Smotrich publicly proposes extreme plans in cabinet
observations.threshold: Open proposal of WMD use or ground invasion → severe US-Israel alliance strain
observations.current_state: Pushed privately, not publicly

observations.label: Israel unilaterally strikes Iranian nuclear facilities
observations.threshold: Any unilateral strike → enters escalation phase
observations.current_state: Has not happened during ceasefire

observations.label: Israeli Knesset election outcome (by 2026-10-27)
observations.threshold: Coalition loses majority → strategic reassessment; coalition holds → current line continues
observations.current_state: ~5 months pre-election; coalition projected at 52 seats (9 short)

variables.label: Iran's Red Line (Attacks on Gulf Energy)
variables.short_desc: Biggest trigger validated by Ras Laffan

cross_page_link.label: → Jump to supply-chain Ras Laffan detail

preconditions.label: 2026-03-18 Ras Laffan proven
preconditions.analysis: Iran launched coordinated missile and drone attacks against Qatar's Ras Laffan (the largest LNG site) + Saudi SAMREF refinery + Kuwait Mina al-Ahmadi refinery + UAE Al Hosn gas field. This is the first proven Iranian red-line strike on Gulf energy infrastructure since the 2023 Beijing Saudi-Iran reconciliation.

preconditions.label: Trump's public extended deterrence
preconditions.analysis: After Ras Laffan, Trump publicly stated: 'Israel won't strike Iran's gas fields again, but if Qatar facilities are struck again, the US will act.' This makes the US security umbrella over the Gulf explicit — Iran must factor 'do it again = US enters' into its calculus.

preconditions.label: IRGC Navy units retain strike capacity
preconditions.analysis: Even after the 12-Day War and Operation Epic Fury — 70% of IRGC ballistic missile launchers destroyed + 92% of naval vessels destroyed — Iran is not fully degraded. The remaining 30% launchers + 8% vessels + abundant small boats sustain selective strike capability.

preconditions.label: Iran's missile inventory back to half pre-war level
preconditions.analysis: Dubowitz (FDD, December 2025) report: Iran's missile production capacity has recovered to 2000–3000 per year within six months of war. Current inventory roughly half of pre-war (~1250–3000 missiles). This sustains Iran's 'selective retaliation' option.

preconditions.label: Gulf states' air defenses partially effective (60–80% interception)
preconditions.analysis: During the Ras Laffan attack, Gulf air defense systems intercepted roughly 60–80% of incoming munitions (specific numbers not disclosed). This creates an Iranian reality of 'strike + partial penetration' — full saturation hard, but selective hits achievable.

mechanism.label: Cyclical ship seizures + selective strikes + proxy indirection
mechanism.analysis: Iran's red-line toolkit: (1) cyclical ship detentions + selective releases (the standard Hormuz play); (2) direct missile/drone strikes on Gulf energy infrastructure (Ras Laffan model); (3) cyber attacks on Gulf financial institutions (Aramco 2012 Shamoon-type); (4) proxy indirection (Houthis / Iraqi militias). Selection logic: Iran knows large-scale strikes invite full US entry, so it picks the 'below full war but above zero' grey zone. Ras Laffan was a typical instance — 5 missiles hit Ras Laffan, clearly a 'demonstration' rather than 'war' level.

consequences.label: No further red-line trigger → talks have room (50–60%)
consequences.analysis: Base case: after Ras Laffan as a one-shot demonstration, Iran enters wait-and-see mode and does not retrigger. This is the rational maximizing path for Iran — keep strike capacity as a negotiating chip but don't use it lightly lest the US fully enter.

consequences.label: Ras Laffan-style repeat → Trump enforces deterrence (20–30%)
consequences.analysis: Iran strikes Gulf energy again. Trump must enforce 'the US will act' or lose credibility. Possible: US strikes IRGC naval base (Bandar Abbas) with limited action + more Hormuz deployment. But this consumes more ammo.

consequences.label: Major escalation → direct US engagement (10–15%)
consequences.analysis: Iran judges 'US won't fully engage anyway' during lame-duck and expands strike — simultaneously hitting multiple Gulf states + US bases. This forces Trump (even in lame-duck) to escalate or pay 'lost Gulf alliance' political cost.

consequences.label: Persistent low-intensity disruption → shipping insurance deteriorates (20–30%)
consequences.analysis: Iran avoids large strikes but continuously disrupts at low intensity — a few seizures, single missiles, proxy indirection. This 'persistent grey' prevents shipping insurance markets from normalizing, forcing DFC to permanently bear reinsurance load. Supply chains under long-term strain.

observations.label: Any new Iranian attack on the Gulf (even if intercepted)
observations.threshold: Any new missile / drone attack on Gulf energy → Trump's deterrence threshold tested
observations.current_state: Not occurred since Ras Laffan (~2 months)

observations.label: IRGC public warning resembling June 2025 pre-strike messaging
observations.threshold: Specific IRGC warning to Gulf state or US base → attack probability rises
observations.current_state: No specific warnings observed

observations.label: Houthi / Iraqi militias' Iran-directed activity level
observations.threshold: Proxy attack frequency rises significantly → Iranian indirect retaliation signal
observations.current_state: Houthi Red Sea attacks continue; Iraqi militias relatively quiet

evolution_phases.label: Pre-Midterm (face-saving deal push)
evolution_phases.description: Trump pushes a face-saving deal. Possible form: Mojtaba accepts partial IAEA inspection + suspends high-level enrichment + ambiguous commitments on proxies. Trump declares 'we ended the Iran nuclear threat.' Vance leads negotiations and absorbs the 'soft' label. Israel does limited unilateral action (assassinations + missile-site strikes) ahead of the 2026-10-27 Knesset election, but doesn't break the deal framework. Brent in the $80–100 range. Hormuz operates in mixed transit mode (10–20% of pre-war flow).
key_events: Vance leads talks and carries the 'soft' label
Israel's limited unilateral action pre-Knesset election

evolution_phases.label: Lame-Duck Phase (Israel scales up unilateral action)
evolution_phases.description: Republicans lose the House (baseline 60% probability) → Trump's domestic politics paralyzed. The Iran deal is challenged by Congress but pushed by executive. If Mojtaba survives he uses the deal to consolidate internal control. Within the lame-duck window Israel judges 'must finish de-capability while Trump is still here' and scales up unilateral action — larger assassinations and strikes. Brent $80–95. The new Democratic House launches investigations of Hegseth and Project 2025 NEA purge.
key_events: GOP loses House (baseline) → Trump lame duck
Iran deal pushed by executive vs. congressional opposition
Israel's lame-duck-window scale-up of unilateral action

evolution_phases.label: 2028 Primary Run-Up + End of Trump's Term
evolution_phases.description: The 2028 Republican primary goes public. Vance / Rubio / Don Jr compete for the succession. The Iran deal may partially collapse — over execution details or Israeli escalation triggering an Iranian breach declaration. Israel takes its largest unilateral action in the final stretch of the lame-duck window. Brent $90–110 (deal collapses) or $75–85 (deal holds).
key_events: Critical moment for whether the Iran deal partially collapses or holds
Israel's largest unilateral action in the final window

evolution_phases.label: Successor Administration Path Branch
evolution_phases.description: The new president's direction shapes the next phase: Democrats may attempt a JCPOA 2.0; Vance has narrow operational room; Rubio may restart escalation; the Don Jr / Carlson camp may push full isolationism, leaving Israel to handle Iran alone.
key_events: Don Jr / Carlson camp wins → full isolationism

worst_case.label: Disintegration + Maritime Anarchy
worst_case.description: 3–5% probability over 24 months, but the destruction would be equivalent to 1929 Great Depression + 1973 oil shock + 2008 financial crisis + 2020 COVID stacked.
triggers: Mojtaba dies suddenly + IRGC cannot quickly produce internal consensus
Israeli large-scale escalation (Smotrich pushes WMD use)
Simultaneous mobilization in Khuzestan + Kurdistan + Baluchestan
China and Russia unwilling/unable to bail Iran out
consequences: IRGC's provincial naval units act autonomously → Strait of Hormuz becomes 'maritime anarchy'
Global industrial output severely contracts → recession
Semiconductor, defense industry, and fertilizer supply chains break simultaneously
monitoring_signals: Inconsistencies among IRGC branches' public statements
Unrest signals in Iran's peripheral regions (Kurdistan, Baluchestan, Khuzestan)
Whether China and Russia would be willing to mount an emergency rescue
